name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحَابًا فَيَبْسُطُهُۥ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ وَيَجْعَلُهُۥ كِسَفًا فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ ۖ فَإِذَآ أَصَابَ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦٓ إِذَا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ (٤٨)
```

```english
It is Allah who sends the winds. Then they generate a cloud, then He spreads it as He wishes in the sky, and forms it into fragments, whereat you see the rain issuing from its midst. Then, when He strikes with it whomever of His servants that He wishes, behold, they rejoice; (48)
```

```arabic
وَإِن كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمُبْلِسِينَ (٤٩)
```

```english
and indeed they had been despondent earlier, before it was sent down upon them. (49)
```

```arabic
فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِ رَحْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ كَيْفَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٥٠)
```

```english
So observe the effects of Allah’s mercy: how He revives the earth after its death! Indeed He is the reviver of the dead, and He has power over all things. (50)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِنْ أَرْسَلْنَا رِيحًا فَرَأَوْهُ مُصْفَرًّا لَّظَلُّوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ يَكْفُرُونَ (٥١)
```

```english
And if We send a wind and they see it turn yellow, they will surely become ungrateful after that. (51)
```

```arabic
فَإِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَلَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٥٢)
```

```english
Indeed you cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs [upon you], (52)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَنتَ بِهَـٰدِ ٱلْعُمْىِ عَن ضَلَـٰلَتِهِمْ ۖ إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٥٣)
```

```english
nor can you lead the blind out of their error. You can make only those hear who have faith in Our signs and have submitted. (53)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the third section of verses presenting arguments by way of the acts of Allah, the Exalted—or if you prefer, the names of His acts. The primary objective of these verses is to argue for the Resurrection (المعاد) — *the return of all beings to Allah for judgment*. Since the core of their denial and rejection was directed toward the Resurrection—and because denying it renders religious laws and divine paths (الشرائع) futile, thereby nullifying monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*—these arguments are followed by a consolation to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to despair of them. He is commanded to occupy himself with calling those who possess an inner readiness for faith (الإيمان), a capacity for Islam (الإسلام), and a submission (التسليم) to the Truth.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **اللَّهُ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً فَيَبْسُطُهُ فِي السَّماءِ كَيْفَ يَشاءُ** “It is Allah who sends the winds and they stir up a cloud, and He spreads it in the sky as He wishes” until the end of the verse. Stirring (الإثارة) means movement and dispersal. Clouds (السحاب) refers to the mist (الغمام). The sky (السماء) refers to the direction of height; everything that is above you and shades you is a "sky." Fragments (الكسف) is the plural of *kisfah*, meaning a piece. The rain (الودق) refers to the drops of rain, and the interstices (الخلال) is the plural of *khillah*, meaning a gap or opening.

The meaning is: Allah is He who sends the winds, which move and disperse a cloud, and He spreads that cloud in the upper regions of the atmosphere as He, the Glorified, wills. He makes it into overlapping and accumulated fragments (قطعات), and you see the raindrops emerging from its gaps. When He causes that rain to fall upon whomsoever He wills of His servants, they suddenly rejoice, for it is the substance of their life and the life of animals and plants.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِنْ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمُبْلِسِينَ** “And though they were despondent before it was sent down to them—before that.” Despondency (الإبلاس) means despair and hopelessness (القنوط).

The pronoun in “sent down” (يُنَزَّلَ) refers to the rain, as does the pronoun in “before that” (مِنْ قَبْلِهِ), according to what has been said. Based on this, “before that” serves as an emphasis (تأكيد) for His saying: “before it was sent down to them.” The benefit of this emphasis—as has been suggested—is to highlight the speed with which human hearts fluctuate from despair to rejoicing. This is because the phrase “before it was sent down to them” could imply a broad span of time, so “before that” was added to indicate immediate proximity and to dispel that possibility.

In *al-Kashshaf*, it is mentioned that His saying “before that” (مِنْ قَبْلِهِ) is a matter of repetition and emphasis, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **فَكانَ عاقِبَتَهُما أَنَّهما فِي النَّارِ خالِدَيْنِ فِيها** “So the outcome for both of them was that they will be in the Fire, to abide in it [forever].” The meaning of emphasis here is to indicate that their time without rain had been prolonged and distant, such that their despair had become entrenched and their despondency protracted; thus, their rejoicing was in proportion to their prior grief. (End of quote).

It has also been said that the pronoun in “before that” (مِنْ قَبْلِهِ) refers to the sending of the winds. The meaning would then be: “And though they were, before the rain was sent down to them—that is, before the sending of the winds—despairing and hopeless.”

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَانْظُرْ إِلى آثارِ رَحْمَتِ اللَّهِ كَيْفَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمُحْيِ الْمَوْتى وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** “So observe the effects of Allah’s mercy: how He revives the earth after its death! Indeed He is the reviver of the dead, and He has power over all things.” Effects (الآثار) is the plural of *athar*, which is what remains after a thing and points toward it, like a footprint or the remains of a building. It is used metaphorically for everything that branches out from something. What is meant by “Allah’s mercy” is the rain descending from the clouds spread by the winds. Its “effects” are what result from the rainfall, such as plants, trees, and fruits, which are themselves the effects of the earth’s life after its death.

For this reason, He said: “So observe the effects of Allah’s mercy: how He revives the earth after its death.” He made the effects of mercy—which is the rain—the manner in which the earth is revived after its death. Thus, the revival of the earth after its death is among the effects of mercy, and the plants, trees, and fruits are among the effects of its life, and they are also among the effects of mercy and the divine governance (التدبير)—a governance that branches out from the creation of winds, clouds, and rain.

His saying: “Indeed He (ذلِكَ) is the reviver of the dead.” The demonstrative pronoun “that” (ذلِكَ) refers to Him, the Exalted, in His capacity as the possessor of mercy, whose effects include the revival of the earth after its death. The use of the distant demonstrative pronoun is for veneration (تعظيم). “The dead” (الموتى) refers to the human dead, or humans and other living beings.

The purpose of His saying: “Indeed He is the reviver of the dead” is to demonstrate the similarity between reviving the dead earth and reviving the dead. In both cases, there is death—the loss of the effects of life from a preserved thing—and life—the renewal of those effects after their loss. Since revival has been realized in the earth and plants, the revival of humans and other living beings is analogous to it. The ruling for analogous cases (الأمثال) regarding what is possible and what is impossible is one and the same. If revival is possible in some of these analogous cases—namely the earth and plants—it must be possible in the others.

His saying: “and He has power over all things” is an affirmation of the aforementioned revival through another explanation, which is the universality of power (عموم القدرة). Power is neither limited nor finite; therefore, it encompasses revival after death. Otherwise, it would necessitate its restriction, whereas it has been posited as absolute and unlimited.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَئِنْ أَرْسَلْنا رِيحاً فَرَأَوْهُ مُصْفَرًّا لَظَلُّوا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ يَكْفُرُونَ** “And if We send a wind and they see it turn yellow, they will surely become ungrateful after that.” The pronoun in “they see it” (فَرَأَوْهُ) refers to the vegetation, understood from the context. His saying “they will surely become” (لَظَلُّوا) is the response to the oath, standing in place of the apodosis. The meaning is: I swear, if We were to send a cold wind that strikes their crops and trees with yellowness, and they were to see it, they would persist after that in being ungrateful for His blessings.

The verse contains a rebuke for their rapid fluctuation between blessing and affliction. When a blessing appears to them, they hasten to rejoice; but when some of what Allah has bestowed of His grace is taken away, they do not hesitate to disbelieve in the established blessings.

It has been said that the pronoun in “they see it” (فَرَأَوْهُ) refers to the cloud, because a cloud, when yellow, does not produce rain. It has also been said it refers to the wind, as it can be treated as both masculine and feminine. Both views are far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَإِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى** “For you cannot make the dead hear” until His saying **فَهُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ** “so they have submitted.” This is a justification for what is understood from the preceding context, as if it were said: Do not occupy yourself with, nor grieve over, these people whose states fluctuate between despondency, rejoicing, and disbelief, and who fail to believe in Our signs or contemplate them. For they are dead, deaf, and blind.

You are not able to make them hear or to guide them. You can only make those hear and guide those who believe in Our signs—that is, those who contemplate these arguments and affirm them—for they are submissive (مسلمون). The explanation of these two verses has already been provided in Surah al-Naml.

## [ Surah al-Rum (30): Verses 54 to 60 ]
